md: When a third line crosses two parallel lines, it creates a beautiful pattern of related angles. Knowing the names — and the rules — lets you fill in any unknown angle once one is known.
The crossing line is called a **transversal**. The two parallel lines stay parallel, and the transversal makes 8 angles where it crosses them (4 at each intersection).

blocks.title: The setup
blocks.body: Picture two horizontal parallel lines $\ell_1$ and $\ell_2$, with a slanted transversal $t$ crossing both. The 8 angles are numbered 1–8 (or labeled). Same-position angles at the two intersections turn out to be equal — that's where the magic comes from.

md: ### The four angle pairs you must know
**Corresponding angles** — same position relative to each intersection (e.g., both upper-right). **They're equal.**
**Alternate interior angles** — between the parallel lines, on opposite sides of the transversal. **Equal.**
**Alternate exterior angles** — outside the parallel lines, on opposite sides of the transversal. **Equal.**
**Co-interior angles** (same-side interior) — between the parallel lines, on the *same* side of the transversal. **Supplementary** (sum to 180°).
Plus the rules from the angle-types lesson:
**Vertical angles** at each intersection (across from each other) are equal.
**Linear pair** angles (next to each other on a straight line) are supplementary.

blocks.title: The key insight
blocks.body: Once you know **one** angle in this configuration, **every other angle is determined**. They're either equal to it (same value) or supplementary to it (180° minus it). That's it — there are only two values among the 8 angles.

md: Two parallel lines are crossed by a transversal. One of the angles measures 65°. What are the other 7?
Half the angles equal **65°** (corresponding, alternate, vertical to the first one). The other half equal $180° - 65° = $ **115°** (linear pair, co-interior). That's it.
Lines $\ell_1 \parallel \ell_2$. Angle 3 (alternate interior with angle 6) is $4x + 10$. Angle 6 is $6x - 30$. Find $x$.
Alternate interior angles are equal: $4x + 10 = 6x - 30 \Rightarrow 40 = 2x \Rightarrow x = 20$.

These rules go both ways. **If a transversal cuts two lines and the corresponding angles are equal, then the lines are parallel.** Same for alternate interior, alternate exterior, etc. This is how we *prove* lines are parallel: show one of the matching angle pairs is equal (or co-interior pair is supplementary).

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: Transversal + parallel lines → 8 angles, only 2 distinct values. **Corresponding, alternate interior, alternate exterior, vertical** → equal. **Co-interior, linear pair** → supplementary. Knowing one angle determines all the others.
